summary:
Eratosthenes Measures the Earth (c. 240 BC) was a landmark achievement of ancient science in which the head of the [[event:1044|Library of Alexandria]] used geometric reasoning and the angle of the sun's shadow to calculate Earth's circumference with remarkable accuracy — a demonstration that ancient science could yield quantitative knowledge of the natural world through systematic observation and mathematics.

Eratosthenes of Cyrene, scholar-librarian at Alexandria, knew that at noon on the summer solstice at Syene (Aswan), the sun was directly overhead (it shone straight down a well without shadow). At Alexandria, simultaneously, the sun cast a shadow at an angle of about 7.2 degrees (1/50th of a circle). Knowing the approximate distance from Alexandria to Syene, Eratosthenes multiplied by 50 to get Earth's circumference — approximately 40,000 km, remarkably close to the modern value of 40,075 km. He also calculated the tilt of Earth's axis, the distance to the Sun and Moon, and made a remarkably accurate map of the known world. His method — using two data points, simple geometry, and proportional reasoning — exemplifies Greek scientific thinking at its best. He coined the term 'geography' and founded the discipline, producing work that influenced cartographers until the Renaissance.

images.caption: 19th century reconstruction of Eratosthenes' map of the known world, c. 194 BC.
